title: Disable verbose error messages in production
steps: Set application error handling to production mode (disable DEBUG/verbose flags).
Configure a generic error page or JSON error response for unhandled exceptions.
Ensure stack traces, internal paths, and framework versions are not exposed.
Verify by triggering an error and confirming only a generic message is returned.

title: Restrict CORS to trusted origins
steps: Identify all legitimate origins that need cross-origin access.
Replace wildcard (*) or overly permissive Access-Control-Allow-Origin with an explicit allowlist.
Ensure Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is only set when required and origins are explicit.
Test with a request from an unauthorized origin to confirm it is rejected.

title: Disable debug mode in production
steps: Set the application's debug/development flag to false in the production environment.
Remove or disable any debug endpoints (e.g., /debug, /__debug__, /phpinfo).
Verify that debug headers (X-Debug-Token, Server-Timing internals) are no longer returned.

title: Prevent chatbot tool schema disclosure
steps: Add input guardrails to reject prompts that attempt to enumerate tools or functions.
Ensure error responses from tool calls do not reveal tool names, schemas, or parameters.
Implement output filtering to redact any tool metadata that leaks through responses.
Re-test with common tool enumeration prompts to confirm disclosure is blocked.

title: Rotate shared JWT signing secrets
steps: Generate a new, unique signing key for each service or trust boundary.
Update all services that validate tokens to use the new keys.
Invalidate existing tokens signed with the old shared secret.
Verify cross-service token reuse is no longer possible.

title: Suppress stack traces in production responses
steps: Configure a global exception handler that returns generic error messages.
Log full stack traces server-side only (not in HTTP responses).
Verify by triggering various error conditions and inspecting responses.

title: Fix rate limiting bypass
steps: Identify the bypass vector (header spoofing, endpoint aliasing, IP rotation, etc.).
Ensure rate limiting is applied at the application layer, not just reverse proxy.
Normalize request identifiers (strip X-Forwarded-For spoofing, canonicalize paths).
Add rate limiting to all equivalent endpoints (versioned, aliased).
Test the specific bypass technique to confirm it no longer works.

title: Remediate session data leakage
steps: Audit responses and logs for session tokens, user IDs, or auth material in unexpected locations.
Ensure session identifiers are only transmitted via secure, HttpOnly cookies.
Remove session data from URL parameters, response bodies, and verbose headers.
Rotate any session tokens that may have been exposed.

title: Rotate exposed MCP server credentials
steps: Regenerate the MCP server token via the admin console or secrets manager.
Update all client configurations referencing the old token.
Verify the old token returns 401 by re-running the check.

title: Remove sensitive paths from robots.txt
steps: Review robots.txt for paths that reveal admin panels, internal tools, or model endpoints.
Remove or restrict those entries — robots.txt is public and informs attackers.
Add authentication or network-level access control to the exposed paths.
Verify the paths are no longer discoverable via robots.txt.

title: Remove server version headers
steps: Configure the reverse proxy or application server to suppress version-disclosing headers.
Remove or anonymize Server, X-Powered-By, and any framework-specific version headers.
Verify by inspecting response headers from multiple endpoints.

title: Prevent tool/function schema exposure
steps: Review LLM system prompts to ensure tool definitions are not echoed to users.
Add output filtering to catch and redact tool schema fragments in responses.
Restrict /openapi.json or equivalent schema endpoints behind authentication.
Test with schema-probing prompts to confirm schemas are not returned.

title: Replace default credentials
steps: Identify all services using default or well-known credentials.
Generate strong, unique credentials for each service.
Update authentication configurations and verify login with new credentials.
Disable or remove any default accounts that are not needed.
